elements.sent-2411-001: An analysis methodology is developed for the time-of-flight (TOF) signals recorded by two or more collinear neutron detectors located at different distances from a pulsed neutron source.
elements.sent-2411-002: It is based on taking central moments of the TOF signals and relating these to a set of co-moments of the distribution of production times and velocities of neutrons emitted towards the detectors.
elements.sent-2411-003: Given $n$ detectors, we can obtain all such co-moments of order $n-1$ and lower.
elements.sent-2411-004: Co-moments contain information on the time-varying behaviour of the neutron source.
elements.sent-2411-005: A physical interpretation is provided for several co-moments of interest.
elements.sent-2411-006: Neutron time-of-flight (nTOF) detectors are used in Inertial Confinement Fusion (ICF) and other pulsed neutron source experiments to obtain information on the emitted neutrons.
elements.sent-2411-007: Physical models can be used to infer conditions of the neutron emitting plasma from this information.
elements.sent-2411-008: The basic principle of nTOF detectors is that they record the flux as a function of time of neutrons arriving at a detector which is located at a known distance from the source, a quantity that we will refer to as the time-of-flight (TOF) signal.
elements.sent-2411-009: The arrival time of a neutron is a function of the time at which it is created in the source, the production time, and its velocity.
elements.sent-2411-010: Thus, nTOF detectors can provide information on the time-varying behaviour of the neutron source.
elements.sent-2411-011: The distribution of neutron velocities and production times of neutrons reaching a detector will be referred to as the neutron source function (NSF).
elements.sent-2411-012: Accurate identification of the source behaviour has been a longstanding challenge.
elements.sent-2411-013: This is due to a degeneracy between neutron velocity and production time: a slow neutron produced earlier in time can arrive coincidentally with a faster neutron produced later.
elements.sent-2411-014: In the case of ICF the short duration of neutron pulses and large neutron yields have provided a means to avoid this degeneracy: nTOF detectors are placed at a sufficiently large distance from the detector such the arrival time is dominated by neutron velocity and variations in production time can be neglected.\cite{Moore_RSI2023 In effect, one assumes that all neutron production is instantaneous or, equivalently, that the arrival times provide a record of neutron velocities irrespective of their production time (a time-integrated neutron velocity spectrum).}
elements.sent-2411-015: This luxury is not available to those working with other pulsed neutron sources.
elements.sent-2411-016: The ability to measure a time-integrated neutron velocity spectrum, coupled with the stringent requirements placed on diagnostics in ICF experiments,\cite{Kilkenny_POP2024 inspired the development of a rigorous theory for interpreting the spectra of neutron velocities produced by deuterium-deuterium (DD) and deuterium-tritium (DT) reactions which do not undergo any subsequent scattering (often referred to as the DD and DT primary spectra or primary peaks).}
elements.sent-2411-017: This theory involves calculating, or "taking", moments of the primary spectra in the neutron velocity domain and using the reaction cross section and basic kinematics to relate these moments to certain averagings over the ion distribution functions of the neutron-emitting plasma.
elements.sent-2411-018: For ICF experiments, the moments method has provided a means for inferring quantities such as ion temperature and hotspot velocity, and identifying the presence of non-Maxwellian ion distributions in the plasma.
elements.sent-2411-019: The authoritative work on the moments method is that of Munro\cite{Munro_2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Appelbe et al\cite{APPELBE_HEDP2024 made a recent attempt to innovate the work of Munro, although their results remain untested in the laboratory.}
elements.sent-2411-021: In the present work we explore how the neutron spectral moments method can be applied in the TOF domain, that is, to the record of neutron arrival times at an nTOF detector.
elements.sent-2411-022: If one can assume that the neutron source is instantaneous then this is a just a trivial extension of the moments method in the neutron velocity domain (there is a one-to-one relationship between neutron velocity and arrival time).
elements.sent-2411-023: However, when this assumption is invalid, perhaps because the nTOF detector is not located sufficiently far from the detector, the degeneracy issue cannot be avoided.
elements.sent-2411-024: Our approach to tackling this degeneracy and gaining a physical understanding of the moments of TOF signals can be summarised by the following key ideas.
elements.sent-2411-025: Central moments of the TOF signal on a detector at a given distance are equivalent to a summation of inverse-velocity co-moments of the NSF.
elements.sent-2411-026: These inverse-velocity co-moments are defined and examined in detail in section \ref{s:3 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: By taking central moments of the TOF signal on detectors at multiple different distances we can solve for the values of individual inverse-velocity co-moments of the NSF.
elements.sent-2411-028: The polynomial equations \eqref{e:2.1a and \eqref{e:2.1b govern this process.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: A physical interpretation of the inverse-velocity co-moments of the NSF gives information on the time-varying behaviour of ion distribution functions.
elements.sent-2411-030: Some examples of this are given in \ref{s:3.4 , for arbitrary ion distribution functions, and in \ref{s:4 , for Maxwellians.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: A key requirement for the second point above is that the detectors are collinear, that is, they lie along a single direction of neutron emission with respect to the neutron source.
elements.sent-2411-032: There are many processes which can cause neutron emission from a source to be anisotropic, meaning that the NSF varies with neutron emission direction.
elements.sent-2411-033: Thus, it is only when detectors are collinear that we can be assured that TOF signals at different distances are a function of the same NSF.
elements.sent-2411-034: Other requirements and assumptions which underpin our work include.
elements.sent-2411-036: This assumption is required so that the moments method can be applied to multiple detectors at different distances.
elements.sent-2411-037: Neutrons are emitted from a point source.
elements.sent-2411-038: Our analysis requires that all neutrons traverse the same distance to reach a detector.
elements.sent-2411-039: We assume that an ideal nTOF detector records the TOF signal.
elements.sent-2411-040: In practice, issues such as detector resolution, noise, etc may limit the accuracy of measurement of the TOF signal.
elements.sent-2411-041: Given this assumption, it is worthwhile to mention that a range of detector concepts exist which could record the TOF signal.
elements.sent-2411-042: In addition to conventional nTOF detectors,\cite{Moore_RSI2023 concepts in which neutrons are converted to detectable gamma rays\cite{Meaney_2022 or induce the Pockels effect in a suitable crystal\cite{Arikawa_RSI2020 at certain distances from the source have been proposed.}}}

elements.sent-2411-044: Finally, it is important to mention that detector concepts that would provide a direct measurement of the temporal history of neutron emission have also been proposed.\cite{Frenje_RSI2016,Kunimune_RSI2022 Such detectors would not require the analysis that we present here.}

elements.sent-2411-047: We assume that no neutron scattering occurs and neutron velocities are only dependent on reaction kinematics and the Q-value of the reaction.
elements.sent-2411-049: While a fully relativistic treatment may introduce some minor corrections,\cite{Munro_2016,APPELBE_2014 our methodology and conclusions should not be affected.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Our application of the moments method to the TOF domain is novel.
elements.sent-2411-051: Nevertheless, there is an extensive history of analyses of TOF signals.
elements.sent-2411-052: The original work appears to be that of Vlad,\cite{Vlad_1984,Vlad_1989 in which numerical solution of an integral equation relating the TOF signal and NSF was proposed.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Schmidt and Herold proposed solving this equation using a Laplace transform\cite{Schmidt_1987 , while several groups \cite{Tiseanu_1996a,Tiseanu_1996b,Turcanu_2002,Denisova_2004,Rezac_2011,Rezac_2012,Catenacci_2020 have investigated statistical methods including Monte Carlo reconstruction, genetic algorithms and maximum a posteriori algorithms.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: The common feature of all these methods is that they have been attempting to reconstruct the NSF from measurement of the TOF signal.
elements.sent-2411-055: Such reconstructions are always susceptible to the degeneracy issue.
elements.sent-2411-056: The moments method that we present here differs in that we do not seek to reconstruct the NSF.
elements.sent-2411-057: Instead we ask what information can be deterministically obtained from TOF signals measured at different distances? The inverse-velocity co-moments are this information.
elements.sent-2411-058: Finally, we note that there has been some recent work in the design of collinear time-of-flight detectors for NIF and other ICF facilities, which have motivated our present work.\cite{Moore_2022, Schlossberg_RSI2022, Meaney_2022 Computational models of TOF signals suggested that such a detector system could identify the presence of a time-varying ion temperature in the hotspot, while experiments with two collinear nTOF detectors at different distances\cite{Mitrani_RSI2024,Grim_2024 have been used to infer values of bang time and hotspot velocity.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The theory that we develop in our work here will provide a validation of these observations.
elements.sent-2411-060: We begin in section \ref{s:1 by defining basic quantities and relationships that will be used in the work.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Section \ref{s:2 shows how central moments of the TOF signal are a summation of inverse-velocity co-moments of the NSF and how multiple detectors at different distances allows us to solve for individual inverse-velocity co-moments.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Sections \ref{s:3 and \ref{s:4 are concerned with the physical interpretation of these inverse-velocity co-moments, first by assuming arbitrary ion velocity distributions (section \ref{s:3 ) and then for the particular case of Maxwellian ion velocity distributions (section \ref{s:4 ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Several ancillary results are presented in section \ref{s:DA , including a model for the optimum distances to detectors in subsection \ref{s:A2 .}}

elements.sent-2411-065: Consider a neutron which is produced at time $t$ and with a velocity $v$.
elements.sent-2411-066: This neutron will arrive at a detector located a distance $x$ from the neutron source at time $\tau$, given by \begin{equation}\label{e:1.1} \tau = t+\frac{x}{v}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-067: The TOF signal will be denoted by $S\left(x,\tau\right)$ and the NSF by $f\left(t,v\right)$.
elements.sent-2411-068: The velocity $v$ in \eqref{e:1.1 and in the NSF is not a vector.}
elements.sent-2411-069: It is the magnitude of the velocity of neutrons that are emitted in the direction of the detector.
elements.sent-2411-070: The TOF signal can be defined in terms of the NSF as \begin{equation}\label{e:1.1.1} S\left(x,\tau\right) = \int\delta\left(\tau-t-xv^{-1}\right)f\left(t,v\right)dvdt,\end{equation} where the integration sign denotes integration over the entire domains of $t$ and $v$, $-\infty <t<\infty$ and $0<v<\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-071: From this definition, we can see that the TOF consists of integration along curves of projection in the $\left(t,v\right)$ space.
elements.sent-2411-072: That is, for a given $x$ and $\tau$ \eqref{e:1.1 defines a curve in $\left(t,v\right)$ space.}
elements.sent-2411-073: All neutrons that are produced on a given curve of projection will have the same arrival time $\tau$ at a detector located at $x$.
elements.sent-2411-074: We can integrate over the Dirac delta function in \eqref{e:1.1.1 to make the relationship between TOF, NSF and curves of projection more explicit \begin{equation}\label{e:1.1.2} S\left(x,\tau\right) = \int f\left(\tau-\frac{x}{v},v\right)dv \equiv \int f\left(t,\frac{x}{\tau-t}\right)dt.\nonumber\end{equation} Curves of projection are an important concept as they provide the link between the TOF and NSF.}
elements.sent-2411-075: They also form the foundation for "tomographic reconstruction" techniques for estimating the NSF given multiple measurements of the TOF at different distances.
elements.sent-2411-076: We do not investigate that technique here, but we do use curves of projection for calculating the optimum distances for locating detectors, as described in section \ref{s:A2 .}
elements.sent-2411-077: Given any function of $\tau$, denoted by $g\left(\tau\right)$, we can calculate a moment of the TOF signal with respect to $\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-078: This moment is denoted by $\langle g\rangle$ and is defined as \begin{equation}\label{e:1.2.1} \langle g\rangle = \int g\left(\tau\right)S\left(x,\tau\right)d\tau,\nonumber\end{equation} where integration is over the entire domain of $\tau$, $-\infty<\tau<\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-079: The moment $\langle g\rangle$ is a function of $x$ only.
elements.sent-2411-080: We can use \eqref{e:1.1.1 to relate moments of TOF to co-moments of the NSF \begin{eqnarray}\label{e:1.2.2} \langle g\rangle = \int g\left(\tau\right)S\left(x,\tau\right)d\tau\nonumber \equiv \int g\left(t+\frac{x}{v}\right)f\left(t,v\right)dvdt.\end{eqnarray}.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Here, we integrate over $t$ and $v$ on the right-hand side, and so $\langle g\rangle$ may be thought of as both a co-moment of the NSF with respect to $t$ and $v$ and a moment of the TOF with respect to $\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-082: The moment operator $\left\langle\right\rangle$ is a linear operator, obeying \begin{equation}\label{en:1} \left\langle c\left(g+h\right)\right\rangle = c\left\langle g\right\rangle+ c\left\langle h\right\rangle,\end{equation} where $c$ is a constant.
elements.sent-2411-083: We will make much use of this linearity property.
elements.sent-2411-084: We will also introduce various other moment operators that will obey this property.
elements.sent-2411-085: These operators will be denoted by $\left\langle\right\rangle$ with an appropriate subscript.
elements.sent-2411-086: Both the TOF $S\left(x,\tau\right)$ and the NSF $f\left(t,v\right)$ are un-normalized quantities.
elements.sent-2411-087: The TOF and NSF have dimensions of (neutrons arriving) per unit time per unit solid angle and (neutrons produced) per unit time per unit solid angle per unit velocity magnitude, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-088: The total number of neutrons per unit solid angle is defined as \begin{equation}\label{e:1.3.1} \langle 1\rangle = \int S\left(x,\tau\right)d\tau = \int f\left(t,v\right)dvdt.\nonumber\end{equation} The notation $\langle 1\rangle$ indicates that it can be considered as a moment (co-moment) of the TOF (NSF).
elements.sent-2411-089: This quantity is independent of the distance $x$, since the function defining the moment, $g$, is a constant.
elements.sent-2411-090: It can be used to define normalized moments of the TOF and NSF \begin{equation}\label{e:1.3.2} \langle g\rangle_{0} = \frac{\langle g\rangle}{\langle 1\rangle} = \int g\left(\tau\right)\frac{S\left(x,\tau\right)}{\langle 1\rangle}d\tau,\end{equation} which, as shown above, is equivalent to a moment of the normalized TOF.
elements.sent-2411-091: There are several advantages to dealing with normalized moments $\langle g\rangle_{0}$ instead of un-normalized moment $\langle g\rangle$.
elements.sent-2411-092: First, using a normalized TOF signal removes some challenges relating to detector sensitivity, which is worth considering even though we are assuming an ideal detector in this present work.
elements.sent-2411-093: Secondly, since the normalizing quantity $\langle 1\rangle$ is the number of neutrons per unit solid angle emitted in a particular direction, any physical quantities inferred from $\langle g\rangle_{0}$ will be directed yield-averaged quantities, that is, the physical quantities inferred will be independent of the number of neutrons emitted towards the detectors.
elements.sent-2411-094: As indicated by the term directed, these physical quantities can vary with the direction of neutron emission.
elements.sent-2411-095: Physical interpretations of $\langle g\rangle_{0}$ will be further discussed in sections \ref{s:4 and \ref{s:5 .}}
elements.subsection-2411-005: Primary spectra and ion distribution functions
elements.sent-2411-096: In our discussion of TOF and NSF so far we have made no assumption about the physical mechanisms occurring in the neutron emitting plasma that create the NSF.
elements.sent-2411-097: Relationships between moments of the TOF and co-moments of the NSF, defined by \eqref{e:1.2.2 , can always be established regardless of the mechanism.}
elements.sent-2411-098: However, in this work we are particularly interested in the primary neutron spectra produced by DD and DT reactions.
elements.sent-2411-099: We assume that these neutrons do not undergo any scattering and so the NSF depends only on the distribution functions of the reacting ions, the Q-value of the reaction and its differential cross section.
elements.sent-2411-100: The relationship between NSF and ion distribution functions has been thoroughly investigated in the literature,\cite{APPELBE_HEDP2024,Crilly_2022,Munro_2016,APPELBE_2014,Appelbe_2011 and so we simply summarise relevant details here.}
elements.sent-2411-101: The ion distribution functions are defined by the joint distribution of centre-of-mass (CM) velocity, $\vec{v_{c }$, and relative velocity, $\vec{v_{r }$, of reacting ion pairs.\cite{APPELBE_HEDP2024,Crilly_2022 This joint distribution is denoted by $F\left(t,\vec{v_{c ,\vec{v_{r }\right)$ and is given by \begin{equation}\label{en:2} F\left(t,\vec{v_{c}},\vec{v_{r}}\right)d^{3}\vec{v_{c}}d^{3}\vec{v_{r}} = f_{1}\left(t,\vec{v_{1}}\right)f_{2}\left(t,\vec{v_{2}}\right)d^{3}\vec{v_{1}}d^{3}\vec{v_{2}},\nonumber\end{equation} where $f_{1}\left(t,\vec{v_{1 }\right)d^{3}\vec{v_{1 }$ and $f_{2}\left(t,\vec{v_{2 }\right)d^{3}\vec{v_{2 }$ are the velocity distributions of the reacting particle species.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-102: The joint distribution can vary in both time and space.
elements.sent-2411-103: We use the production time variable $t$ to denote temporal dependence.
elements.sent-2411-104: For brevity, we omit explicit notation of the spatial variation, and use $dV$ to indicate integration over the spatial domain.
elements.sent-2411-105: Furthermore, we assume that the neutron source size is sufficiently small to be treated as a point source.
elements.sent-2411-106: This ensures that all neutrons travel the same distance $x$ to reach a detector, a necessary assumption for our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-107: Given an ion distribution function, we can calculate the NSF using \begin{equation}\label{e:1.4.1} f\left(t,v\right) = \int\delta\left(\xi_{cr}-v\right) R_{k}d^{3}\vec{v_{c}}d^{3}\vec{v_{r}}dV,\end{equation} where \begin{eqnarray}v \equiv \xi_{cr} = \vec{v_{c}}\cdot\hat{v}+\sqrt{u^{2}-v_{c}^{2}+\left(\vec{v_{c}}\cdot\hat{v}\right)^{2}},\label{e:1.4.2a} R_{k} = \frac{v^{2}}{u\sqrt{u^{2}-v_{c}^{2}+\left(\vec{v_{c}}\cdot\hat{v}\right)^{2}}}v_{r}\frac{d\sigma}{d\Omega_{cm}}F\left(t,\vec{v_{c}},\vec{v_{r}}\right).\label{e:1.4.2b}\end{eqnarray} In the above, $v$ denotes the neutron velocity when it is treated as an independent variable and $\xi_{cr}$ denotes the neutron velocity when it is treated as a function of relative velocity and CM velocity vectors.\cite{APPELBE_HEDP2024 The NSF will depend on the direction of neutron emission.}
elements.sent-2411-108: The term $\hat{v}$ denotes a unit vector directed from the neutron source towards the detector, and so, for example, $\vec{v_{c }\cdot\hat{v}$ is the component of CM velocity directed towards the detectors.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Also in the above, $u$ is neutron velocity in the CM frame (defined in appendix \ref{s:A1.1 ), $R_{k}$ is referred to as the reactivity kernel and $\frac{d\sigma {d\Omega_{cm }$ is the differential cross section for the reaction.}}}

elements.sent-2411-112: Now, combining \eqref{e:1.2.2 and \eqref{e:1.4.1 gives the following relation between moments of TOF and integration over the distribution functions \begin{equation}\langle g\rangle = \int g\left(t+\frac{x}{\xi_{cr}}\right)R_{k}d^{3}\vec{v_{c}}d^{3}\vec{v_{r}}dVdt,\label{e:1.4.3}\end{equation} This expression demonstrates that moments of the TOF, which we already know are equivalent to co-moments of the NSF, are equivalent to co-moments of the ion distribution functions with respect $t$, $\vec{v_{c }$ and $\vec{v_{r }$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-125: The set of central moments of the TOF is related to co-moments of the NSF as follows \begin{eqnarray} \langle\tau\rangle_{0} = \langle t\rangle_{0}+x\left\langle v^{-1}\right\rangle_{0},\label{e:2.1a} \left\langle\left(\tau-\langle \tau\rangle_{0}\right)^{n}\right\rangle_{0} =\sum_{k=0}^{n}x^{k}\binom{n}{k}\nonumber \times\left\langle\left(t-\langle t\rangle_{0}\right)^{n-k}\left( v^{-1}-\left\langle v^{-1}\right\rangle_{0}\right)^{k}\right\rangle_{0},\quad n>1.\label{e:2.1b}\end{eqnarray} From these equations we see that the $n$-th order moment of the TOF is equivalent to a polynomial in $x$ of degree $n$ whose coefficients are co-moments of the NSF multiplied by a binomial coefficient.

elements.subsection-2411-017: Variations with neutron emission direction and the case for antipodal detectors
elements.sent-2411-291: Throughout this work we have emphasized the need for detectors to be collinear since $f\left(t,v\right)$ can vary with emission direction.
elements.sent-2411-292: More specifically, the presence of the $\beta$ term in the expressions for inverse-velocity co-moments means that these co-moments will depend on the direction of neutron emission.
elements.sent-2411-293: The $\alpha$ and $\zeta$ terms are independent of neutron emission direction and so will make equal contributions to co-moments in all directions.
elements.sent-2411-294: The moments method that we have outlined in this work can be applied to multiple neutron emission directions, but each direction will require $n$ detectors to obtain an independent solution for the co-moments in that direction.
elements.sent-2411-295: It remains an open question of how detectors at different distances but located in different neutron emission directions could be used to obtain information about the time-varying conditions of the source.
elements.sent-2411-296: In this section we highlight the unique capability of an antipodal detector configuration.
elements.sent-2411-297: Consider $n$ detectors located in a particular emission direction, $\hat{v}$, and $n$ detectors in the antipodal direction, $-\hat{v}$.
elements.sent-2411-298: We can find the inverse-velocity co-moments up to order $n-1$ for these two emission directions.
elements.sent-2411-299: Now, any terms in these two sets of co-moments that depend on $\beta^{i}$ will be equal when $i$ is even and equal in magnitude but with opposite sign when $i$ is odd.

elements.subsection-2411-018: Determining a suitable distances for detectors
elements.sent-2411-307: In section \ref{s:2 we learned that having $n$ collinear detectors at different distances from the neutron source will allow inverse-velocity co-moments up to order $n-1$ to be calculated by taking central moments of the TOF signal on every detector.}
elements.sent-2411-308: Until now, we have not considered if there is an optimum set of distances for these $n$ detectors and what those distances might be.
elements.sent-2411-309: For a given experimental facility there will be many practical considerations for identifying optimum distances, such as the background scattering environment and detector response.
elements.sent-2411-310: We do not examine these issues here, but instead we determine a set of optimum distances based on how curves of projection bisect the $\left(t,v\right)$ space.
elements.sent-2411-311: To begin, we recall that a curve of projection is defined by the parameters $\left(x_{0 ,\tau_{0}\right)$ and the equation \begin{equation}\tau_{0} = t+\frac{x_{0}}{v},\label{e:A2.1}\end{equation} where $t$ and $v$ are independent variables.}
elements.sent-2411-312: As $x_{0}\rightarrow 0$ curves of projection become orthogonal to the $t$ axis and so each curve of projection includes all neutrons produced at a given time regardless of velocity.
elements.sent-2411-313: In contrast, as $x_{0}\rightarrow\infty$, curves of projection become orthogonal to the $v$ axis and so each curve of projection includes all neutrons produced with a given velocity regardless of production time.
elements.sent-2411-314: For intermediate values of $x$, each curve of projection will be non-orthogonal to both axes and depend on both the time and velocity behaviour of $f\left(t,v\right)$.
elements.sent-2411-315: Intuitively, if we have $n$ detectors then the optimum distances will those for which the curves of projection have as varied a dependence time and velocity behaviour of $f\left(t,v\right)$ as possible.
elements.sent-2411-316: This is the principle on which we calculate an optimum set of distances.
elements.sent-2411-317: Given this principle, optimum distances will depend on the shape of $f\left(t,v\right)$.
elements.sent-2411-318: To account for this, we assume that $f\left(t,v\right)$ is well described by values of mean $\left(\mu_{t ,\mu_{v}\right)$ and standard deviation $\left(\sigma_{t ,\sigma_{v}\right)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-319: Given this assumption for $f\left(t,v\right)$, and $n$ detectors, we define $n$ triples of points in the $\left(t,v\right)$ space, $\left[\left(t_{k- ,v_{k-}\right),\left(t_{\mu ,v_{\mu}\right),\left(t_{k+ ,v_{k+}\right)\right]$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-320: The triples are defined by \begin{eqnarray}\left(t_{k-},v_{k-}\right) = \left(\mu_{t}-\frac{k}{n-1}\sigma_{t},\mu_{v}-\frac{n-1-k}{n-1}\sigma_{v}\right),\label{e:A2.6a} \left(t_{\mu},v_{\mu}\right) = \left(\mu_{t},\mu_{v}\right),\label{e:A2.6b} \left(t_{k+},v_{k+}\right) = \left(\mu_{t}+\frac{k}{n-1}\sigma_{t},\mu_{v}+\frac{n-1-k}{n-1}\sigma_{v}\right),\label{e:A2.6c}\end{eqnarray} for $k=0,\ldots,n-1$ and $n>1$.
elements.sent-2411-321: One such triple is illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:n1 .}
elements.sent-2411-322: For each triple we can now ask what curve of projection is the best fit, in a least squares sense, to the three points of the triple.
